sanskrit: अनन्तविजयं राजा कुन्तीपुत्रो युधिष्ठिरः । नकुलः सहदेवश्च सुघोषमणिपुष्पकौ ॥
hindiTranslation: कुन्तीपुत्र राजा युधिष्ठिर ने अनन्तविजय नामक शंख बजाया तथा नकुल और सहदेव ने सुघोष और मणिपुष्पक नामक शंख बजाये ॥
englishTranslation: King Yudhishthira, son of Kunti, blew the Anantavijaya; Nakula and Sahadeva blew the Sughosha and Manipushpaka.
essence: The war begins to answer back in sound.
speaker: Sanjaya
simpleMeaning: Yudhishthira, Nakula, and Sahadeva blow their conches. The verse adds to the battle scene and names their sacred war horns.
simpleInsight: The battlefield is filling with sound, and both sides are being drawn into the moment.
detailedMeaning: On Kurukshetra, the Pāṇḍava brothers stand ready beside Krishna and Arjuna. After Krishna and Arjuna sound their conches, Yudhishthira, Nakula, and Sahadeva answer with their own. The verse keeps building the charged scene before the fighting begins.
modernRelevance: The room goes quiet before the announcement. One voice follows another, and everyone feels the pressure rise before a hard beginning.
translationLiteral: अनन्तविजयं (Anantavijaya) / राजा (king) / कुन्तीपुत्रः (son of Kunti) / युधिष्ठिरः (Yudhishthira) / नकुलः (Nakula) / सहदेवः (Sahadeva) / च (and) / सुघोषमणिपुष्पकौ (Sughosha and Manipushpaka)
insight: It shifts attention from individual heroes to the full force gathering on one side.
